title: Can Data Be Unfair? Auditing Algorithms for Bias
description: Students audit a hiring dataset for proxy bias, design a data policy, and evaluate the fairness-versus-accuracy tension in machine learning systems.
learningObjectives: Identify hidden bias in a dataset and explain how it can affect model outcomes.
Explain how proxy variables transfer discriminatory patterns without explicitly naming protected characteristics.
Write a data policy specifying excluded features and required additions for fairer model training.
Explain why fixing bias in machine learning often requires retraining from new data rather than deleting one rule.
fullDescription: Age group: 12-17. Duration: 30 minutes. SLO alignment: Domain A (21A), Domain B (22B). Learning goal: students can audit data for bias, detect proxy-variable mechanisms, and design practical policy interventions while recognizing trade-offs between predictive performance and fairness.

roleModel.name: Joy Buolamwini
roleModel.summary: MIT researcher who documented major facial-recognition error gaps across demographic groups and catalyzed product and policy changes.
roleModel.whyItMatters: Her work established bias auditing as core engineering practice, not optional ethics add-on.

teacherInstructions.setup: Write vocabulary and spark question on the board. Prepare hiring-audit dataset for Phase 4.
steps: Phase 1 (0-2 min): Ask whether AI trained on historical data can avoid repeating historical mistakes. Capture both sides quickly.
Phase 1 hook: discuss Amazon hiring-model case where gender was not explicit but historical hiring patterns were learned.
Phase 2 (2-8 min): Proxy variable mini-case using polo, sailing magazine, and second home as misleading indicators.
Phase 2 escalation: connect postcode to socioeconomic and racial segregation patterns and ask how hidden discrimination can be detected.
Phase 3 (8-13 min): Contrast rule-based bias fixes with ML bias remediation. In ML, bias is distributed across many learned weights.
Phase 3 pressure question: discuss organizational incentives when scrapping a biased model is expensive.
Phase 4 (13-24 min): Dataset audit challenge in groups using hiring records A to E.
Round 1 (4 min): feature audit for proxy risks including postcode, commute, hobbies, graduation year, and sample-size limits.
Round 2 (5 min): write data policy with excluded features and required additions, including causal mechanism for each exclusion.
Round 3 (3 min): fairness-versus-accuracy dilemma after proxy removals reduce predictive performance.
Phase 5 (24-28 min): discussion on proxy legitimacy, intent, and governance when biased systems are currently deployed.
Exit reflection (28-30 min): students explain how bias can emerge without explicit intent.
discussionPrompts: How do we distinguish harmful proxy use from contextually valid signal use, if at all?
Does beneficial proxy use (for example postcode bonus for disadvantaged applicants) change ethical status?
If a deployed model is biased, should it be shut down immediately or run during replacement, and who decides?
What obligations exist to inform people affected by known model bias?
Can any purely technical fix solve filter bubbles without reducing personalization quality?
tips: Push students to explain mechanisms, not just label features as biased.
Ask who is absent from the dataset, not only what variables are present.
Use disagreement in Round 3 as evidence of mature reasoning, not confusion.
Reinforce that fairness auditing is iterative and outcome-based, not checkbox compliance.
Link proxy-bias insights back to Lesson 5 and fraud-model fairness from Lesson 6.

teacherGuide: Core message: ML bias often lives in data and correlations, not explicit discriminatory code. Effective auditing requires examining proxy mechanisms, representation gaps, error-rate disparities, and governance decisions under business pressure. Keep focus on engineering accountability: measure, document, intervene, and reassess.

studentExercises.title: Exercise 1 - Proxy Variable Detection
studentExercises.description: Analyze simple feature sets to identify hidden selection criteria and explain the mechanism behind proxy bias.
instructions: Review three hiring features with social-class signal.
Identify protected characteristics indirectly targeted.
Explain why no explicit protected field is needed for biased outcomes.
Provide one real-world proxy candidate beyond the examples.
State one way to test for harmful proxy effects.
successCriteria: Students identify at least one valid proxy mechanism.
Students articulate indirect pathway from feature to unfair outcome.
Students propose one measurable audit approach.

studentExercises.title: Exercise 2 - Hiring Dataset Audit
studentExercises.description: Audit feature risks and representation gaps in a small hiring dataset used to train a model.
instructions: Inspect each feature for proxy risk and fairness impact.
Flag representation gaps and missing populations.
Assess sample-size adequacy for generalization.
Prioritize top three audit findings by expected harm.
Prepare one short justification per finding.
successCriteria: Group identifies obvious and subtle proxies.
Group notes missing-group issue, not only variable issue.
Group ranks findings using consequence-based reasoning.

studentExercises.title: Exercise 3 - Data Policy Draft
studentExercises.description: Write an actionable policy with excluded variables, rationale mechanisms, and required data additions.
instructions: List excluded features and explain precise unfairness pathway.
List required additions to improve representativeness.
Define one monitoring metric for post-deployment fairness.
State review cadence and ownership role.
Test policy against fairer-but-less-accurate dilemma and defend choice.
successCriteria: Policy includes mechanism-level justifications, not generic claims.
Policy addresses both exclusion and inclusion strategy.
Policy includes concrete monitoring and governance assignments.

studentExercises.title: Exit Reflection - Unintended Bias
studentExercises.description: Write one sentence explaining how algorithmic bias can emerge without explicit intent.
instructions: Complete: An algorithm can be biased without anyone intending it to be, because _______.
Reference either training data, proxy variables, or representation gaps.
Include one concrete class example.
Share one response aloud.
successCriteria: Student links bias source to data-generation process.
Student avoids framing intent as sole cause of harm.
Student references a concrete mechanism from lesson activities.

studentChallenges.title: Fairness vs Accuracy Trade-off
studentChallenges.description: Decide between a less accurate fairer model and a more accurate model that reinforces existing institutional patterns.
instructions: Choose a deployment stance and justify stakeholders impacted.
Define one condition under which you would halt deployment.
Define one condition under which temporary deployment is acceptable.
Specify who should approve final decision and why.
successCriteria: Student distinguishes business incentives from public interest.

studentChallenges.title: Filter Bubble Systems Question
studentChallenges.description: Evaluate whether personalization systems can reduce polarization without degrading individual user experience.
instructions: Describe one technical intervention to reduce filter bubbles.
Predict one user-experience cost of that intervention.
Propose who should set platform trade-off policy.
State one metric for democratic-health impact and one for user satisfaction.
successCriteria: Student identifies tension between individual and collective outcomes.
Student recognizes that technical and governance choices are coupled.
